speaker: interviewee
text: Aye, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: December 1, 1996, interview with Mr. William Cohn, Dallas, Texas, United States of America. Mr. Cohn, where we just left, I asked you how you had put your life back together once you came back to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Palestine-- the first time when I came to Palestine [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was, for me, a shock, a culture shock. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were-- although we never were, neither my father, nor my family, or I were anytime real German patriots. And I  have an aversion, actually, against patriotism. Because the patriots in Germany were mostly-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] their attitude was a national vanity. And, right or wrong, you know, 'Deutschland, Deutschland uber Alles,' you know, Germany, Germany were everything. You know? And-- the superiority of Germany and all this. I have developed a real-- and then between the two wars-- from the-- from the end  of World War I until the Nazi-- Nazi regime, there was a strong-- a strong movement of pacifism, which was only in the-- only within the democratically inclined population. Yes, the intellectuals, not all, but many intellectuals, many of them were Jews, professionals, working people that had-- that had suffered terribly losses during the war and after the revolution. It was-- it-- it was-- the revolution was Communist aims, but eventually was-- was then controlled by the Social Democrats, who-- who established a democratic republic. And all these nationalistic people, many of them wanted to have the monarchy back. Although the monarchy had-- had led them into the disastrous World War. And of course, the Jews were blamed for the loss of the war. It was called the stab in the back, the [SPEAKING GERMAN]. The stab in the-- the stab in the back. So you stab with knife, you know, in the back, meant the Jews, the internationally Jewry. You know. And everything was blamed on the Jews. And-- and blamed by these-- by these mostly middle class right wing people but not-- but not by the working people. And those working people were-- 95% of our customers were working people and-- and socialists.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- I have to come back to things. I-- on the first of April, 1933-- what was that, two months after the Nazi take over? There was declared a day of boycott of all Jewish establishments and stores. So in the morning, two Nazis-- wore brown shirts-- positioned themselves in the-- at the entrance to our store to prevent people from going in and-- and-- and buying something.

speaker: interviewee
text: But all the day long, people, our customers, came in and asked my parents just to make them up a-- a-- a package. So-- not to put nothing in there, just make them package, as if they had bought something, just a show of defiance to those Nazis. And then in the evening, at 6 o'clock, when the last shift of Nazis left, one of our non-Jewish saleswomen took a pail of water and a scrubber, and she scrubbed those two spots where these-- these two Nazis had stood, like mad. You  know, like the scrubbing of dirt in-- in front of anybody in the street that wanted to look at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were these kind of people. They were-- they-- and as I said, there was a really strong movement of pacifism in Germany. And I-- I was very much influenced by that. And-- and also, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] personality-wise, I always was against-- against injustices. Even today, I can't take-- I can't-- I can't-- I have a hard time to cope with injustice and-- and violence. And so my father, we all, we were not German patriots, and-- but we-- but we felt we belonged in Germany. You know, we were Germans. You know, we belonged there. And I loved the country. I loved the the-- the-- everything about it. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was brought up. And I-- I went to school. And I got the German education. And-- so-- when-- when-- when the turn came, you know, when the Nazis took over, we couldn't understand that all of a sudden, you know, we didn't belong there. But I understood it pretty soon. But the older generation, many, not all, but many of the older generation did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came to Palestine, first of all, I was shocked by the scenery. I was always very affected by-- by scenery, by landscape, you know, by-- by countryside. And we were used to, you know, green forests and-- and hills and mountains, especially after-- after I was in Northern Italy in those beautiful mountains. And when I came there, it was all dry, and rocks, and-- and-- and barren. You know? It was the first shock that I [? got in ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then, and then when I came into a kibbutz, into that area, you know, it was all like that. You know, it was a lot of barren ground. And even when there was vegetation, it was dry. You know, in summer it was [? florid. ?] So summers were dry and heat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The mountains they were-- they were-- they said, [INAUDIBLE], look over there. Those are the mountains. I said, mountains? [LAUGHS] They were hills to me. So-- and then the whole social life there didn't appeal to me. Also, as I said before, if I would have chosen a different kibbutz, it would have been different. But there, we were all left to our own-- on our own. You know, we were new. What did we know? Even our luggage, we had our luggage was in the Port of Jaffa. Nobody-- nobody-- nobody gave a damn about that. You know? And fortunately, I had some money with me. My parents [INAUDIBLE]. But I didn't want to take at lot of money. My father wanted to give me a [INAUDIBLE] mark. But I don't want any money. I am kibbutzic. I am pioneer. I don't want any money. And-- but I had a few-- a few pounds. You know? So we were about six people, six people. And they all had their luggage in the Port of Jaffa. And-- but nobody cared that we could-- could get our luggage. So I-- so I took my money, you know, and we asked the driver of the kibbutz a favor. He went every day. He drove into Tel Aviv. And we asked him a favor, to go to the place where our luggage was and bring us back the luggage. And this had to paid for. It to be, the luggage, you know, it had to be paid for, you know, for-- to-- to release-- to release our-- our luggage, we had to pay to the-- to the-- shipping company. You know? So I took all my money. The others didn't have any money. So I took all the money, and we-- we went with a truck, I think, to Tel Aviv. And we got all the luggage out. So [INAUDIBLE] and there was nobody to-- to talk to us. In the dining room, there was no communication between-- and that-- the kibbutz had two parts. There was the original settlement that was down near-- near a village, [INAUDIBLE]. And some time before we came, they had moved the bulk of the kibbutz to an exposed area.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the Jewish National Fund had-- had given them land to settle on and to-- and for living in for fields, yes. And that was the new settlement, and that was across from those two hostile villages. And there they sent all the young people. And on there, where we were, where we came, they had all the old people, sick people, and-- and all the, you know, deadwood. In fact, it was the time I was there-- in the beginning, we-- we were quartered in-- in an empty chicken coop. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's where we-- where we-- where we stayed in the beginning, first few weeks. Because the kibbutz had been bankrupt. So the chickens-- they took all the chickens away. So the chicken coop was empty. So it was a-- was a place to-- to put us. And eventually, I got a little tent for-- one man, yeah. Next to my tent was a little wooden shed. And the-- the one-- one man lived in there. And one morning, he was found. He had hanged himself. Another time, one girl, she was crazy. And she used to run-- run naked into the village. So they had to run and-- and bring her back all the time. Another guy took a big nail, stuck it through his throat. So this was the atmosphere around there. And then old people, you know, old parents with beards, long beards, it was strange for us anyway. So there was a very depressing, very depressing atmosphere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war, and all the experiences that you've had, how were you able to put your life back together in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my ideal of becoming an artist [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was destroyed, right, by-- by the time I had to leave the school. And so I had to forget about that. And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And then, I just had to try and make a living. When I was in kibbutz, you know, with all these unskilled jobs, you know, digging-- digging trenches, or working in these-- in these [INAUDIBLE]. And it was all very monotonous and mentally deadening work, yeah. And physically very heavy, very-- very hard. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I left the kibbutz because I didn't feel I belonged to it. You know? I never got a feeling of being part of it or being at home. And-- and I wasn't the only one. There were about 80 people at the time that-- that left. So when I came to Haifa, and I was on my own, again, because of the difficulties of-- of finding work and whatever-- and I mean daily by, you know, know one work, one day. One day is up here. And another day another kind of work there, and you know. So it was-- it was nothing that gave me any feeling of stability and of belonging there. And-- so I never really felt well in Palestine [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until-- until-- until the war came. I joined the army. In the meantime, my sister was here in America, and she wanted me to come here. So I made up my mind. After the war, if I survive, I would leave Palestine. And there was no other place to go than here, you know? When did you go to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1955. But before that, [INAUDIBLE], after the end of the war, I got married. And I told my wife that I want [INAUDIBLE], and I wanted to leave Palestine. So she said, OK, if you want to leave. She-- she did not think of leaving because she was really-- she was-- she was more realistic than I. She had a very hard time also there, very hard time. But she was more, more practical than-- than I was. I was kind of naive. You know, when I came, I was naive. So I was-- I was disillusioned easily. But my wife came-- came from a very poor family. And she actually left Austria to get away from the poverty and came-- and went to Palestine. And then she went into-- she was sent into an agricultural school for women. And also, there a hard time. And-- but she was tough. It was really-- And-- and then, she-- she founded, with some other-- some other eight-- eight people I think, she founded a new kibbutz, a new settlement in the Jordan Valley. And then when the war broke out, her parents were left in Vienna. And she-- so she wanted to-- to get her parents out. And her-- her sister said-- one of her sisters said, we have to leave the kibbutz. Because we have to try and make money for our parents to come, to get them here. So she left the kibbutz. Then-- but-- and then she-- one day, she stood in Tel Aviv from, I don't know, 4 o'clock in the morning or all day long, I think, at the consulate, [INAUDIBLE] consulate, to ask to-- to get a visa, an entrance visa. And she couldn't get one. So she didn't-- they get her parents out either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and then when she was already out of the kibbutz, she stayed outside. And she worked [INAUDIBLE]. She worked also as a-- as a maid and in a hotel, as a chambermaid, you know, [INAUDIBLE] and then as a waitress. And-- and-- you know, she was a very hard worker. So-- so, she-- but she didn't think of leaving Palestine. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when we got married, I said, I don't want to stay here. She said, if you want to-- if you don't want to stay, then we won't. And we will leave. But let's try first. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did both you and your wife work when you were in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: If we worked here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you both work when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I-- I-- I looked for work right away. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the first two years, the first job I found in the United  States was as a-- as a helper on a-- on a-- on a truck. It was those delivery trucks for Coca-Cola. And there I wrenched my back. I was lifting one  of those cases. I wrenched my back. So that came to an end. And then I had all sorts of other jobs. I think five different jobs. And the last job I had was on a laundry-- laundry truck. It was in Chicago. And the route I had was in the-- one of the worst ghettos in Chicago. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Laundry drivers and the other delivery drivers, they were always-- they were always sitting ducks for holdup men, you know? And of course, I was held up also at gunpoint. That and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyways, that-- that-- that job was very hard. It was very-- at that time, I was only 40, 42 years old and carrying out that heavy laundry, you know, bag-- bag-- big bags of laundry up to-- to the fourth, fifth floor. And it-- and it was a heart attack. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was after-- after we were two years here, and we had two kids, and we had nothing. We were standing there with no resources, no savings or anything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we thought of going back to Palestine, to Israel at that time [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody gave me an idea to-- to make a portfolio and go out to advertising studios and see if I could get-- get into the-- into the art field, into the advertising art. And I didn't get into that, but I-- I got a job in a printing press and-- and became a lithographer. Yes? And that was really the best job I ever had.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the only setback on that job was the hostility of my coworkers. For my coworkers, I was I was the new immigrant, a-- a Jew, and a Communist. You know, [INAUDIBLE]. Because I never could-- that was one of my problems. I never could keep my mouth shut. I always have to speak my mind. And it happened there too. And-- And-- But I loved the work. I liked the work very much. But with this also work was tension. You know, because in advertising, it's always rush. Everything is rush. And that is a kind of work that you have to rush, but you have be very, very accurate. So I was very, very accurate, but I wasn't very, very fast. But others were fast and not so accurate. So-- so it had to be made over. Their jobs had to be made over. And it cost more money than I [INAUDIBLE], who worked slower. And-- and the jobs were all right. Anyway, I worked there for 19 years. And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after 17 or 18 years, I think it was, I had another heart attack, and a very, very, very almost fatal heart attack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I went back to work. And after two years, I was again close to another heart attack. And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a-- a neighbor persuaded me to have an-- have an open heart-- have open heart surgery, which I didn't want to. But after a long talk with my wife, I-- I agreed to have it. So-- it was in the beginning where heart attacks wasn't that-- they're not like today, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really-- I was tired. I was tired of life. That is true. But then after the talk with my wife, I-- I felt that she still needed me. So I agreed to have the operation. And after the operation, I went back to work. And two months later I was fired. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Partly because I-- I had-- I couldn't work so much overtime. I-- I-- I was still too tired, you know, from the operation. Partly because of that, but-- but more than that, more than that, was-- were the intrigues of my dear colleagues, my coworker who finally succeeded in having me fired.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna need to take a tape break.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
